titleStmt.title: 412. Telegram From the Embassy in Jordan to the to the Department of State

div.head: 412. Telegram From the Embassy in Jordan to the Department of State Source: Department of State, Central Files, 320/9—1860. Secret; Priority. Repeated to London, Cairo, Tel Aviv, Beirut, Damascus, Jerusalem, and USUN.
div.p: 451. Embtel 446.In telegram 446 from Amman, September 17, Kocher reported that Hussein had decided to head the Jordanian Delegation to the United Nations. While in the United States, Hussein stated that he hoped to be able to “renew acquaintance with old friends.” Kocher commented, in passing this information to the Department, that while Hussein’s decision would obviously complicate an already crowded schedule at the United Nations, he felt it was a positive development to be encouraged in that it meant that Hussein had decided to move away from a possible military confrontation with Syria and work through the United Nations toward a solution to the problem. (Ibid., 320/9–1760; included in the microfiche supplement) Immediately after telling me he intended go to UNGA in October, King Hussein summoned in British Ambassador Johnston and UN Ambassador Spinelli and gave him same information. Later in evening three of us got together to compare notes. Both Johnston and Spinelli believe King’s visit to UN a healthy development. Spinelli states that King said he intended visit New York to confer with SYG re redress Majali assassination plot but Hussein likewise stressed he also has “right to take problem to GA” which obviously reflects pressure on SYG to produce some results for Jordan in current problem with Syria. As for Spinelli, he will probably also appear in New York “since everyone else going to UN”.

Question remains concerning future of troop concentrations near border. During my interview with King I asked him whether he intended keep troops near border during time he was at UN and suggested advisability of removing at least some units back to previous positions. King said this was most difficult. One of his reasons for build up had been to offer safety valve to his people and if troops now ordered away from border without apparent redress for assassination plot it would weaken him in eyes of army. Therefore, he believed it preferable to leave troops in present positions and let them undergo extended maneuvers and training “for some time” and then gradually return them to previous positions, assuming of course that his trip to UN produced some positive results. Johnston had brought up related question with King concerning possible impulsive decision on part of CINC during Hussein absence in New York, but King had given reassurances this point.

Comment: Decision on King’s part at end UN in effect focuses attention on that organization as potential mediator between Jordan and UAR. Conceivable Hussein may tire of lengthy political processes New York and still decide on some unilateral action his part, but every reason to expect tempers will progressively cool during next few weeks. As occasion offers Johnston and I will try to impress on King reality of UN mechanism, as Spinelli is doing, so that Hussein will less likely be suddenly or sharply disappointed. Rifai and Musa Nasir, shrewd judge of UN processes, should be helpful in this regard.
